title: The Umbrella Stand at the Library Entrance
text_zh: 我们小区旁边的图书馆门口有一个伞架，铁制的，漆已经掉了大半。每到下雨天，伞架上就会出现各种各样的伞：透明的、碎花的、公司发的那种黑色折叠伞。有意思的是，总有几把伞一直没人来取。时间长了，那些被遗忘的伞就成了公共财产——谁要是出门忘带伞，就顺手拿一把。图书馆的管理员王阿姨跟我说，她观察了好几年，发现一个规律：越是便宜的伞，越容易被人认领回去；反而是那些看起来还不错的伞，往往被丢在那里最久。「大概是因为用便宜伞的人更在意每一件东西吧，」她这样总结。我想了想，觉得她说得有道理。后来我也忘了一把伞在那里，是朋友送的，质量挺好。我犹豫了一下要不要回去拿，最后还是算了。因为我发现，有些东西一旦离开了你的手，就自然而然地变成了别人的故事。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū pángbiān de túshūguǎn ménkǒu yǒu yī gè sǎnjià, tiě zhì de, qī yǐjīng diào le dàbàn. Měi dào xiàyǔ tiān, sǎnjià shàng jiù huì chūxiàn gè zhǒng gè yàng de sǎn: tòumíng de, suìhuā de, gōngsī fā de nà zhǒng hēisè zhédié sǎn. Yǒu yìsi de shì, zǒng yǒu jǐ bǎ sǎn yīzhí méi rén lái qǔ. Shíjiān cháng le, nàxiē bèi yíwàng de sǎn jiù chéng le gōnggòng cáichǎn——shuí yàoshi chūmén wàng dài sǎn, jiù shùnshǒu ná yī bǎ. Túshūguǎn de guǎnlǐyuán Wáng āyí gēn wǒ shuō, tā guānchá le hǎo jǐ nián, fāxiàn yī gè guīlǜ: yuè shì piányi de sǎn, yuè róngyì bèi rén rènlǐng huíqu; fǎn'ér shì nàxiē kàn qǐlái hái búcuò de sǎn, wǎngwǎng bèi diū zài nàlǐ zuì jiǔ. 「Dàgài shì yīnwèi yòng piányi sǎn de rén gèng zàiyì měi yī jiàn dōngxi ba,」tā zhèyàng zǒngjié. Wǒ xiǎng le xiǎng, juéde tā shuō de yǒu dàolǐ. Hòulái wǒ yě wàng le yī bǎ sǎn zài nàlǐ, shì péngyou sòng de, zhìliàng tǐng hǎo. Wǒ yóuyù le yīxià yào búyào huíqù ná, zuìhòu háishi suàn le. Yīnwèi wǒ fāxiàn, yǒuxiē dōngxi yīdàn líkāi le nǐ de shǒu, jiù zìrán'érrán de biànchéng le biéren de gùshi.
text_en: There is an umbrella stand at the entrance of the library next to our housing complex. It's made of iron, and most of the paint has already flaked off. On rainy days, all sorts of umbrellas appear on it: transparent ones, floral-patterned ones, the black folding kind companies hand out. The interesting thing is that a few umbrellas always go unclaimed. Over time, the forgotten umbrellas become communal property—if anyone heads out and forgets to bring one, they just grab one on their way. The library attendant, Auntie Wang, told me she'd been observing for years and noticed a pattern: the cheaper the umbrella, the more likely someone would come back for it; whereas the nicer-looking ones tended to sit there the longest. 'Probably because people who use cheap umbrellas care more about every single thing they own,' she concluded. I thought about it and felt she had a point. Later I also forgot an umbrella there—a gift from a friend, quite good quality. I hesitated for a moment about whether to go back for it, but in the end decided not to. Because I realized that once some things leave your hands, they naturally become part of someone else's story.

questions.q_zh: 王阿姨观察到的「规律」是什么？
questions.q_en: What 'pattern' did Auntie Wang observe?

options.text: 便宜的伞更容易被人取回，贵的伞反而没人认领
options.pinyin: piányi de sǎn gèng róngyì bèi rén qǔ huí, guì de sǎn fǎn'ér méi rén rènlǐng
options.text_en: Cheap umbrellas are more likely to be retrieved, while expensive ones go unclaimed

options.text: 下雨天图书馆的客人更多
options.pinyin: xiàyǔ tiān túshūguǎn de kèrén gèng duō
options.text_en: The library has more visitors on rainy days

options.text: 所有的伞最终都会被人拿走
options.pinyin: suǒyǒu de sǎn zuìzhōng dōu huì bèi rén ná zǒu
options.text_en: All umbrellas eventually get taken

options.text: 年轻人比老年人更容易忘记伞
options.pinyin: niánqīngrén bǐ lǎoniánrén gèng róngyì wàngjì sǎn
options.text_en: Young people are more likely to forget umbrellas than elderly people

questions.q_zh: 作者最后没有回去取伞，根本原因是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the fundamental reason the author didn't go back for the umbrella?

options.text: 伞的质量不好，不值得
options.pinyin: sǎn de zhìliàng bù hǎo, bù zhíde
options.text_en: The umbrella was poor quality and not worth it

options.text: 他觉得物品一旦离手就自然归属了别人
options.pinyin: tā juéde wùpǐn yīdàn lí shǒu jiù zìrán guīshǔ le biéren
options.text_en: He felt that once an item leaves your hands it naturally belongs to someone else

options.text: 他不想让王阿姨觉得不好意思
options.pinyin: tā bù xiǎng ràng Wáng āyí juéde bù hǎoyìsi
options.text_en: He didn't want to embarrass Auntie Wang

options.text: 他又买了一把新伞
options.pinyin: tā yòu mǎi le yī bǎ xīn sǎn
options.text_en: He bought a new umbrella

questions.q_zh: 这篇文章主要探讨的是什么主题？
questions.q_en: What theme does this passage primarily explore?

options.text: 图书馆的社区功能
options.pinyin: túshūguǎn de shèqū gōngnéng
options.text_en: The community function of libraries

options.text: 物品在人与人之间的流转与归属感
options.pinyin: wùpǐn zài rén yǔ rén zhī jiān de liúzhuǎn yǔ guīshǔgǎn
options.text_en: How objects circulate between people and the sense of belonging

options.text: 雨天的不便
options.pinyin: yǔtiān de búbiàn
options.text_en: The inconvenience of rainy days

options.text: 如何选购一把好伞
options.pinyin: rúhé xuǎngòu yī bǎ hǎo sǎn
options.text_en: How to choose a good umbrella

title: The Sound of the Vegetable Vendor's Scale
text_zh: 住在老小区的好处之一，是楼下每天早上七点都会来一辆三轮车，卖菜的老陈会把各种蔬菜摆在车上。他用的是那种老式的杆秤，黄铜的秤砣，竹制的秤杆。每次称完菜，秤砣碰到秤盘的时候，会发出一声清脆的响。这个声音我已经听了五年。后来超市越开越多，来老陈这里买菜的人越来越少了。有一天我下楼买菜，发现只剩我一个顾客。老陈一边称菜一边说：「现在的人都去超市了，在那里什么都有，又干净。」我问他为什么不也去超市打工。他笑了笑说：「超市用电子秤，没声音。」我当时没明白他的意思。后来有一天早上我醒来，发现楼下很安静，老陈没来。之后他再也没出现过。我这才意识到，那个秤盘的声音对他来说，大概不只是做生意的工具，而是一种证明自己还在这里的方式。声音消失以后，这条街的早晨就少了一种温度。
text_pinyin: Zhù zài lǎo xiǎoqū de hǎochu zhī yī, shì lóu xià měi tiān zǎoshang qī diǎn dōu huì lái yī liàng sānlúnchē, mài cài de lǎo Chén huì bǎ gè zhǒng shūcài bǎi zài chē shàng. Tā yòng de shì nà zhǒng lǎoshì de gǎnchèng, huángtóng de chèngtuó, zhú zhì de chènggǎn. Měi cì chēng wán cài, chèngtuó pèng dào chèngpán de shíhou, huì fāchū yī shēng qīngcuì de xiǎng. Zhège shēngyīn wǒ yǐjīng tīng le wǔ nián. Hòulái chāoshì yuè kāi yuè duō, lái lǎo Chén zhèlǐ mǎi cài de rén yuè lái yuè shǎo le. Yǒu yī tiān wǒ xià lóu mǎi cài, fāxiàn zhǐ shèng wǒ yī gè gùkè. Lǎo Chén yībiān chēng cài yībiān shuō: 「Xiànzài de rén dōu qù chāoshì le, zài nàlǐ shénme dōu yǒu, yòu gānjìng.」Wǒ wèn tā wèishéme bù yě qù chāoshì dǎgōng. Tā xiào le xiào shuō: 「Chāoshì yòng diànzǐ chèng, méi shēngyīn.」Wǒ dāngshí méi míngbai tā de yìsi. Hòulái yǒu yī tiān zǎoshang wǒ xǐng lái, fāxiàn lóu xià hěn ānjìng, lǎo Chén méi lái. Zhīhòu tā zài yě méi chūxiàn guò. Wǒ zhè cái yìshi dào, nàge chèngpán de shēngyīn duì tā lái shuō, dàgài bù zhǐ shì zuò shēngyì de gōngjù, ér shì yī zhǒng zhèngmíng zìjǐ hái zài zhèlǐ de fāngshì. Shēngyīn xiāoshī yǐhòu, zhè tiáo jiē de zǎochen jiù shǎo le yī zhǒng wēndù.
text_en: One advantage of living in an old residential compound is that every morning at seven a tricycle pulls up downstairs, and Old Chen the vegetable vendor arranges all his produce on the cart. He uses a traditional beam scale—a brass weight, a bamboo balance arm. Each time he finishes weighing vegetables, the weight striking the pan makes a crisp ring. I'd been hearing that sound for five years. Later, as more and more supermarkets opened, fewer people came to buy from Old Chen. One day I went downstairs to buy vegetables and found I was the only customer left. While weighing my order, Old Chen said: 'Everyone goes to the supermarket now. They have everything there, and it's clean.' I asked why he didn't go work at a supermarket too. He smiled and said: 'Supermarkets use electronic scales. No sound.' I didn't understand what he meant at the time. Then one morning I woke up and found it quiet downstairs—Old Chen hadn't come. After that he never appeared again. Only then did I realize that the sound of the scale pan, for him, was probably not just a tool for doing business, but a way of proving he was still here. After the sound disappeared, the mornings on this street lost a kind of warmth.

questions.q_zh: 老陈说「超市用电子秤，没声音」，他真正想表达的是什么？
questions.q_en: When Old Chen says 'Supermarkets use electronic scales—no sound,' what is he really expressing?

options.text: 他觉得电子秤不够准确
options.pinyin: tā juéde diànzǐ chèng búgòu zhǔnquè
options.text_en: He thinks electronic scales aren't accurate enough

options.text: 杆秤的声音是他身份认同和存在感的一部分
options.pinyin: gǎnchèng de shēngyīn shì tā shēnfèn rèntóng hé cúnzàigǎn de yī bùfen
options.text_en: The sound of the beam scale is part of his identity and sense of existence

options.text: 他讨厌现代技术
options.pinyin: tā tǎoyàn xiàndài jìshù
options.text_en: He dislikes modern technology

options.text: 他不会使用电子秤
options.pinyin: tā bú huì shǐyòng diànzǐ chèng
options.text_en: He doesn't know how to use electronic scales

questions.q_zh: 作者说「这条街的早晨就少了一种温度」，是指什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author mean by 'the mornings on this street lost a kind of warmth'?

options.text: 天气变冷了
options.pinyin: tiānqì biàn lěng le
options.text_en: The weather got colder

options.text: 老陈的存在给街道带来的人情味消失了
options.pinyin: lǎo Chén de cúnzài gěi jiēdào dài lái de rénqíngwèi xiāoshī le
options.text_en: The human warmth that Old Chen's presence brought to the street disappeared

options.text: 超市的空调让温度降低了
options.pinyin: chāoshì de kōngtiáo ràng wēndù jiàngdī le
options.text_en: The supermarket's air conditioning lowered the temperature

options.text: 邻居们都搬走了
options.pinyin: línjūmen dōu bān zǒu le
options.text_en: All the neighbors moved away

questions.q_zh: 这篇文章最核心的主题是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the most central theme of this passage?

options.text: 传统商业被现代商业取代的经济分析
options.pinyin: chuántǒng shāngyè bèi xiàndài shāngyè qǔdài de jīngjì fēnxī
options.text_en: An economic analysis of traditional commerce being replaced by modern commerce

options.text: 日常声音承载的存在感与社区纽带
options.pinyin: rìcháng shēngyīn chéngzài de cúnzàigǎn yǔ shèqū niǔdài
options.text_en: The sense of existence and community bonds carried by everyday sounds

options.text: 老式秤比电子秤更好
options.pinyin: lǎoshì chèng bǐ diànzǐ chèng gèng hǎo
options.text_en: Traditional scales are better than electronic ones

options.text: 城市发展带来的噪音问题
options.pinyin: chéngshì fāzhǎn dài lái de zàoyīn wèntí
options.text_en: Noise problems caused by urban development

title: The Night Bus at 11:40
title_zh: 十一点四十的夜班公交
text_zh: 城市里的末班公交总有一种特别的气氛。我坐的那路是328路，末班车晚上十一点四十从火车站出发。车上的乘客大多是加班回来的上班族、刚收摊的小商贩，还有几个总是坐到终点站再折返的老人。司机姓刘，开了这条线十二年。他有个习惯：到每一站，不管有没有人下车，都会多等五秒钟。有人问过他为什么，他说：「这个点了，万一有人在后面跑着追呢？多等几秒又不费什么。」有一次我坐在前排，看见一个年轻女孩在终点站前一站下了车，走了几步又回头，对刘师傅鞠了一躬。刘师傅愣了一下，然后笑着摆了摆手。后来我才知道，那个女孩刚从外地来这个城市找工作，前几个月每天加班到很晚，全靠这趟末班车才能回到住的地方。她说刘师傅多等的那五秒钟，让她觉得这座城市还有人愿意等她。有些善意不需要多大的力气，五秒钟就够了。
text_pinyin: Chéngshì lǐ de mòbān gōngjiāo zǒng yǒu yī zhǒng tèbié de qìfēn. Wǒ zuò de nà lù shì sān'èrbā lù, mòbānchē wǎnshang shíyī diǎn sìshí cóng huǒchēzhàn chūfā. Chē shàng de chéngkè dàduō shì jiābān huílái de shàngbānzú, gāng shōu tān de xiǎo shāngfàn, hái yǒu jǐ gè zǒng shì zuò dào zhōngdiǎnzhàn zài zhéfǎn de lǎorén. Sījī xìng Liú, kāi le zhè tiáo xiàn shí'èr nián. Tā yǒu gè xíguàn: dào měi yī zhàn, bùguǎn yǒu méi yǒu rén xià chē, dōu huì duō děng wǔ miǎozhōng. Yǒu rén wèn guò tā wèishéme, tā shuō: 「Zhège diǎn le, wànyī yǒu rén zài hòumiàn pǎo zhe zhuī ne? Duō děng jǐ miǎo yòu bú fèi shénme.」Yǒu yī cì wǒ zuò zài qiánpái, kànjiàn yī gè niánqīng nǚhái zài zhōngdiǎnzhàn qián yī zhàn xià le chē, zǒu le jǐ bù yòu huítóu, duì Liú shīfu jū le yī gōng. Liú shīfu lèng le yīxià, ránhòu xiào zhe bǎi le bǎi shǒu. Hòulái wǒ cái zhīdào, nàge nǚhái gāng cóng wàidì lái zhège chéngshì zhǎo gōngzuò, qián jǐ gè yuè měi tiān jiābān dào hěn wǎn, quán kào zhè tàng mòbānchē cái néng huí dào zhù de dìfang. Tā shuō Liú shīfu duō děng de nà wǔ miǎozhōng, ràng tā juéde zhè zuò chéngshì hái yǒu rén yuànyì děng tā. Yǒuxiē shànyì bù xūyào duō dà de lìqi, wǔ miǎozhōng jiù gòu le.
text_en: The last bus of the night in a city always has a particular atmosphere. The route I take is the 328, the last bus departing from the train station at 11:40 PM. Most passengers are office workers coming back from overtime, small vendors who've just packed up their stalls, and a few elderly people who always ride to the terminal and then turn around. The driver's surname is Liu; he's driven this route for twelve years. He has a habit: at every stop, regardless of whether anyone is getting off, he waits an extra five seconds. Someone once asked him why, and he said: 'At this hour, what if someone's running to catch the bus behind us? Waiting a few seconds doesn't cost anything.' Once I was sitting in the front row and saw a young woman get off one stop before the terminal. She walked a few steps, then turned back and bowed to Driver Liu. He was startled for a moment, then smiled and waved his hand. Later I learned that the girl had just come from out of town to look for work in this city. For the first few months she worked overtime every night until very late, and relied entirely on this last bus to get home. She said that those five extra seconds Driver Liu waited made her feel that this city still had someone willing to wait for her. Some kindness doesn't require much effort—five seconds is enough.

questions.q_zh: 刘师傅每站多等五秒钟的原因是什么？
questions.q_en: Why does Driver Liu wait an extra five seconds at each stop?

options.text: 公司规定必须等
options.pinyin: gōngsī guīdìng bìxū děng
options.text_en: Company regulations require it

options.text: 他担心深夜有人正在追赶公交车
options.pinyin: tā dānxīn shēnyè yǒu rén zhèngzài zhuīgǎn gōngjiāochē
options.text_en: He worries that someone might be chasing the bus late at night

options.text: 他想让车上的乘客多休息一会儿
options.pinyin: tā xiǎng ràng chē shàng de chéngkè duō xiūxi yīhuìr
options.text_en: He wants the passengers on the bus to rest a bit more

options.text: 路上红灯太多，他需要调整时间
options.pinyin: lù shàng hóngdēng tài duō, tā xūyào tiáozhěng shíjiān
options.text_en: There are too many red lights and he needs to adjust timing

questions.q_zh: 年轻女孩为什么向刘师傅鞠躬？
questions.q_en: Why did the young woman bow to Driver Liu?

options.text: 刘师傅帮她找到了工作
options.pinyin: Liú shīfu bāng tā zhǎo dào le gōngzuò
options.text_en: Driver Liu helped her find a job

options.text: 末班车和他的耐心等待让她在陌生城市感受到温暖
options.pinyin: mòbānchē hé tā de nàixīn děngdài ràng tā zài mòshēng chéngshì gǎnshòu dào wēnnuǎn
options.text_en: The last bus and his patient waiting made her feel warmth in an unfamiliar city

options.text: 她要离开这座城市了
options.pinyin: tā yào líkāi zhè zuò chéngshì le
options.text_en: She was about to leave this city

options.text: 刘师傅没有收她的车票钱
options.pinyin: Liú shīfu méiyǒu shōu tā de chēpiào qián
options.text_en: Driver Liu didn't charge her for the fare

questions.q_zh: 文章结尾的「五秒钟就够了」表达了什么含义？
questions.q_en: What does 'five seconds is enough' at the end of the passage convey?

options.text: 公交车不需要等太久
options.pinyin: gōngjiāochē bù xūyào děng tài jiǔ
options.text_en: Buses don't need to wait too long

options.text: 微小的善举就能给人巨大的温暖
options.pinyin: wēixiǎo de shànjǔ jiù néng gěi rén jùdà de wēnnuǎn
options.text_en: A tiny act of kindness can give someone enormous warmth

options.text: 城市生活节奏太快
options.pinyin: chéngshì shēnghuó jiézòu tài kuài
options.text_en: The pace of city life is too fast

options.text: 刘师傅应该等更长时间
options.pinyin: Liú shīfu yīnggāi děng gèng cháng shíjiān
options.text_en: Driver Liu should wait even longer

title: The Retired Postman's Route
text_zh: 老赵退休三年了，但他每天早上还是六点半出门，骑着那辆绿色的自行车，沿着他送了三十年信的路线转一圈。邻居们一开始以为他是在锻炼身体，后来才发现他是真的在「巡视」——看看谁家的信箱满了没人取，哪栋楼的门锁坏了，路边的行道树是不是需要修剪。居委会的小李说，老赵比他们还清楚这片区域的情况。有一次，老赵发现三号楼的张奶奶连续三天没取报纸，就去敲门，发现张奶奶摔倒了，起不来。要不是老赵，后果不堪设想。我问老赵，退休了为什么还要每天走这条路。他想了一会儿说：「走了三十年，这条路上的每棵树、每个信箱、每扇门我都认识。突然不走了，就好像把它们丢下了。」他顿了顿又说：「其实也不是它们需要我，是我需要它们。每天走这一圈，我才觉得自己还有点用处。」那一刻我突然明白了，有些人的价值感不是来自头衔或收入，而是来自一条走了无数遍的路。
text_pinyin: Lǎo Zhào tuìxiū sān nián le, dàn tā měi tiān zǎoshang háishi liù diǎn bàn chūmén, qí zhe nà liàng lǜsè de zìxíngchē, yánzhe tā sòng le sānshí nián xìn de lùxiàn zhuǎn yī quān. Línjūmen yī kāishǐ yǐwéi tā shì zài duànliàn shēntǐ, hòulái cái fāxiàn tā shì zhēn de zài 「xúnshì」—— kàn kan shuí jiā de xìnxiāng mǎn le méi rén qǔ, nǎ dòng lóu de ménsuǒ huài le, lùbiān de xíngdàoshù shì bú shì xūyào xiūjiǎn. Jūwěihuì de xiǎo Lǐ shuō, lǎo Zhào bǐ tāmen hái qīngchu zhè piàn qūyù de qíngkuàng. Yǒu yī cì, lǎo Zhào fāxiàn sān hào lóu de Zhāng nǎinai liánxù sān tiān méi qǔ bàozhǐ, jiù qù qiāo mén, fāxiàn Zhāng nǎinai shuāi dǎo le, qǐ bù lái. Yào búshi lǎo Zhào, hòuguǒ bùkān shèxiǎng. Wǒ wèn lǎo Zhào, tuìxiū le wèishéme hái yào měi tiān zǒu zhè tiáo lù. Tā xiǎng le yīhuìr shuō: 「Zǒu le sānshí nián, zhè tiáo lù shàng de měi kē shù, měi gè xìnxiāng, měi shàn mén wǒ dōu rènshi. Tūrán bù zǒu le, jiù hǎoxiàng bǎ tāmen diū xià le.」Tā dùn le dùn yòu shuō: 「Qíshí yě bú shì tāmen xūyào wǒ, shì wǒ xūyào tāmen. Měi tiān zǒu zhè yī quān, wǒ cái juéde zìjǐ hái yǒu diǎn yòngchu.」Nà yī kè wǒ tūrán míngbai le, yǒuxiē rén de jiàzhígǎn bú shì lái zì tóuxián huò shōurù, ér shì lái zì yī tiáo zǒu le wúshù biàn de lù.
text_en: Old Zhao retired three years ago, but every morning he still goes out at 6:30, riding that green bicycle of his, making one loop along the route where he delivered mail for thirty years. The neighbors first assumed he was exercising, but later realized he was genuinely 'patrolling'—checking whether anyone's mailbox was overflowing, whether any building's door lock was broken, whether the roadside trees needed trimming. Xiao Li from the neighborhood committee said Old Zhao knows the state of this area better than they do. Once, Old Zhao noticed that Grandma Zhang in Building 3 hadn't collected her newspaper for three days straight, so he went and knocked on her door—and found she had fallen and couldn't get up. If not for Old Zhao, the consequences would have been unthinkable. I asked Old Zhao why he still walks this route every day after retirement. He thought for a moment and said: 'I walked it for thirty years. Every tree on this road, every mailbox, every door—I know them all. To suddenly stop would feel like abandoning them.' He paused, then added: 'Actually, it's not that they need me. It's that I need them. Walking this loop every day is the only way I feel I still have some use.' In that moment I suddenly understood: some people's sense of worth comes not from titles or income, but from a road they've walked countless times.

questions.q_en: What is Old Zhao's real purpose for going out every morning after retirement?

title: The Bench Nobody Sits On
text_zh: 公园里有很多长椅，但靠近东门的那把几乎从来没有人坐。不是因为它坏了——它的状态其实比大部分长椅都好，绿色的油漆还很新，木板也没有开裂。问题出在位置上：它正对着一面高墙，既看不到湖，也看不到花坛，左边是垃圾桶，右边是公共厕所的入口。坐在那里，视野里只有灰色的水泥墙和来来回回去厕所的人。我之所以注意到这把长椅，是因为有一天傍晚我走遍了整个公园，所有的椅子都坐满了人，只有它空着。我坐下来，才发现了一个意外的好处：因为对着高墙，风被挡住了，冬天坐在那里其实很暖和。而且因为没人坐，特别安静。我开始每天下班后专门来这里坐十分钟。渐渐地，我甚至喜欢上了那面水泥墙——下午的时候，夕阳的光会从墙顶慢慢滑下来，颜色从橙色变成粉红色，最后变成紫灰色。我想，有时候一个地方被所有人忽略，反而成了它最大的优点。
text_pinyin: Gōngyuán lǐ yǒu hěn duō chángyǐ, dàn kàojìn dōngmén de nà bǎ jīhū cónglái méiyǒu rén zuò. Bú shì yīnwèi tā huài le——tā de zhuàngtài qíshí bǐ dàbùfen chángyǐ dōu hǎo, lǜsè de yóuqī hái hěn xīn, mùbǎn yě méiyǒu kāiliè. Wèntí chū zài wèizhì shàng: tā zhèng duì zhe yī miàn gāo qiáng, jì kàn bú dào hú, yě kàn bú dào huātán, zuǒbiān shì lājītǒng, yòubiān shì gōnggòng cèsuǒ de rùkǒu. Zuò zài nàlǐ, shìyě lǐ zhǐ yǒu huīsè de shuǐní qiáng hé lái lái huí huí qù cèsuǒ de rén. Wǒ zhī suǒyǐ zhùyì dào zhè bǎ chángyǐ, shì yīnwèi yǒu yī tiān bàngwǎn wǒ zǒu biàn le zhěnggè gōngyuán, suǒyǒu de yǐzi dōu zuò mǎn le rén, zhǐ yǒu tā kōng zhe. Wǒ zuò xiàlái, cái fāxiàn le yī gè yìwài de hǎochu: yīnwèi duì zhe gāo qiáng, fēng bèi dǎng zhù le, dōngtiān zuò zài nàlǐ qíshí hěn nuǎnhuo. Érqiě yīnwèi méi rén zuò, tèbié ānjìng. Wǒ kāishǐ měi tiān xiàbān hòu zhuānmén lái zhèlǐ zuò shí fēnzhōng. Jiànjiàn de, wǒ shènzhì xǐhuan shàng le nà miàn shuǐní qiáng——xiàwǔ de shíhou, xīyáng de guāng huì cóng qiáng dǐng mànmàn huá xiàlái, yánsè cóng chéngsè biànchéng fěnhóngsè, zuìhòu biànchéng zǐ huīsè. Wǒ xiǎng, yǒu shíhou yī gè dìfang bèi suǒyǒu rén hūlüè, fǎn'ér chéng le tā zuì dà de yōudiǎn.
text_en: There are many benches in the park, but the one near the east gate almost never has anyone sitting on it. Not because it's broken—its condition is actually better than most benches, with fresh green paint and no cracked boards. The problem is its location: it faces a high wall directly, with no view of the lake or the flower beds. To the left is a garbage bin, to the right is the entrance to the public restroom. Sitting there, all you see is a grey cement wall and people going back and forth to the restroom. The reason I noticed this bench was that one evening I walked through the entire park and every seat was taken—except this one. I sat down and discovered an unexpected benefit: because it faces the high wall, the wind is blocked, and in winter it's actually quite warm there. And because nobody sits there, it's exceptionally quiet. I started coming here specifically for ten minutes after work each day. Gradually, I even grew fond of that cement wall—in the afternoon, the light of the setting sun slides slowly down from the top, the color shifting from orange to pink, and finally to a purple-grey. I think sometimes a place being ignored by everyone is precisely what makes it wonderful.

title: My Grandmother's Two Accents
text_zh: 外婆在上海住了五十多年，普通话说得已经很标准了，带一点上海味道，但不仔细听的话根本听不出来。可每次接到老家湖南亲戚的电话，她的口音会在几秒钟之内切换回湖南话，语速变快，声调也完全不一样。小时候我觉得这很有趣，像看一个人变魔术。长大以后我慢慢理解了：语言不只是交流的工具，它还是一种情感的容器。外婆用普通话过她在上海的日常生活——买菜、跟邻居聊天、看电视新闻。但她用湖南话装着另一个自己——那个十八岁离开家乡、坐了三天火车来到上海的女孩。有一年过年，外婆喝了点酒，突然用湖南话唱了一首歌，谁也听不懂歌词，但那个旋律让她哭了。后来她擦了擦眼泪说：「这是我妈教我的，我怕哪天忘了。」那一刻我理解了一件事：有些人并不是在两种口音之间切换，而是在两个自己之间来回走动。而连接这两个自己的，不过是一条看不见的声音的线。
text_pinyin: Wàipó zài Shànghǎi zhù le wǔshí duō nián, pǔtōnghuà shuō de yǐjīng hěn biāozhǔn le, dài yīdiǎn Shànghǎi wèidao, dàn bù zǐxì tīng de huà gēnběn tīng bù chūlái. Kě měi cì jiē dào lǎojiā Húnán qīnqi de diànhuà, tā de kǒuyīn huì zài jǐ miǎozhōng zhī nèi qiēhuàn huí Húnánhuà, yǔsù biàn kuài, shēngdiào yě wánquán bù yīyàng. Xiǎo shíhou wǒ juéde zhè hěn yǒuqù, xiàng kàn yī gè rén biàn móshù. Zhǎng dà yǐhòu wǒ mànmàn lǐjiě le: yǔyán bù zhǐ shì jiāoliú de gōngjù, tā hái shì yī zhǒng qínggǎn de róngqì. Wàipó yòng pǔtōnghuà guò tā zài Shànghǎi de rìcháng shēnghuó——mǎi cài, gēn línjū liáotiān, kàn diànshì xīnwén. Dàn tā yòng Húnánhuà zhuāng zhe lìng yī gè zìjǐ——nàge shíbā suì líkāi jiāxiāng, zuò le sān tiān huǒchē lái dào Shànghǎi de nǚhái. Yǒu yī nián guònián, wàipó hē le diǎn jiǔ, tūrán yòng Húnánhuà chàng le yī shǒu gē, shuí yě tīng bù dǒng gēcí, dàn nàge xuánlǜ ràng tā kū le. Hòulái tā cā le cā yǎnlèi shuō: 「Zhè shì wǒ mā jiào wǒ de, wǒ pà nǎ tiān wàng le.」Nà yī kè wǒ lǐjiě le yī jiàn shì: yǒuxiē rén bìng bú shì zài liǎng zhǒng kǒuyīn zhī jiān qiēhuàn, ér shì zài liǎng gè zìjǐ zhī jiān láihuí zǒudòng. Ér liánjiē zhè liǎng gè zìjǐ de, búguò shì yī tiáo kàn bú jiàn de shēngyīn de xiàn.
text_en: My grandmother lived in Shanghai for over fifty years. Her Mandarin had become quite standard, with a slight Shanghai flavor—but unless you listened carefully, you wouldn't notice. Yet every time she received a call from relatives back in Hunan, her accent would switch back to Hunanese within seconds, her speech speeding up, the tones entirely different. As a child I found this amusing, like watching someone perform a magic trick. Growing up, I gradually understood: language is not just a tool for communication—it's also a vessel for emotion. Grandmother used Mandarin for her daily life in Shanghai—buying vegetables, chatting with neighbors, watching the news. But she kept another self inside Hunanese—the girl who left her hometown at eighteen and rode a train for three days to reach Shanghai. One Spring Festival, after drinking a bit of wine, she suddenly sang a song in Hunanese. Nobody could understand the lyrics, but the melody made her cry. Afterward she wiped her tears and said: 'My mother taught me this one. I'm afraid of forgetting it someday.' In that moment I understood something: some people aren't switching between two accents—they're walking back and forth between two selves. And what connects those two selves is nothing more than an invisible thread of sound.

options.text_en: Chinese dialects are disappearing

title: The Office Fridge War
text_zh: 我们办公室的冰箱曾经引发过一场持续了三个月的「战争」。起因很简单：有人吃了别人的酸奶。被吃酸奶的同事小王贴了一张纸条在冰箱上：「请不要动我的酸奶，谢谢。」第二天，酸奶又少了一盒。小王又贴了一张：「我的酸奶上面有名字，请认清再拿。」第三天，有人在纸条下面回了一句：「你的酸奶太好喝了，下次请买两盒。」从此，冰箱门成了整个办公室的留言板。有人抱怨剩饭味道太大，有人要求把臭豆腐扔掉，有人提议大家AA制买水果。最精彩的是，行政部的张姐每周五下班前会把冰箱里超过三天的东西全部清理掉，她管这叫「冰箱审判日」。有一次她不小心扔了老板的咖啡豆，全办公室都替她捏了一把汗。结果老板什么都没说，只是第二天又买了一袋咖啡豆，上面用记号笔写了四个大字：「请勿扔掉」。从那以后，冰箱上的纸条越来越少了——不是问题解决了，而是大家都学会了在放东西之前先写上自己的名字和日期。有时候，一场混乱最终能催生出秩序，只是过程不太体面。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen bàngōngshì de bīngxiāng céngjīng yǐnfā guò yī chǎng chíxù le sān gè yuè de 「zhànzhēng」. Qǐyīn hěn jiǎndān: yǒu rén chī le biéren de suānnǎi. Bèi chī suānnǎi de tóngshì xiǎo Wáng tiē le yī zhāng zhǐtiáo zài bīngxiāng shàng: 「Qǐng búyào dòng wǒ de suānnǎi, xièxie.」Dì'èr tiān, suānnǎi yòu shǎo le yī hé. Xiǎo Wáng yòu tiē le yī zhāng: 「Wǒ de suānnǎi shàngmiàn yǒu míngzì, qǐng rèn qīng zài ná.」Dì sān tiān, yǒu rén zài zhǐtiáo xiàmiàn huí le yī jù: 「Nǐ de suānnǎi tài hǎo hē le, xià cì qǐng mǎi liǎng hé.」Cóngcǐ, bīngxiāng mén chéng le zhěnggè bàngōngshì de liúyánbǎn. Yǒu rén bàoyuàn shèngfàn wèidao tài dà, yǒu rén yāoqiú bǎ chòu dòufu rēng diào, yǒu rén tíyì dàjiā AA zhì mǎi shuǐguǒ. Zuì jīngcǎi de shì, xíngzhèng bù de Zhāng jiě měi zhōuwǔ xiàbān qián huì bǎ bīngxiāng lǐ chāoguò sān tiān de dōngxi quánbù qīnglǐ diào, tā guǎn zhè jiào 「bīngxiāng shěnpàn rì」. Yǒu yī cì tā bù xiǎoxīn rēng le lǎobǎn de kāfēidòu, quán bàngōngshì dōu tì tā niē le yī bǎ hàn. Jiéguǒ lǎobǎn shénme dōu méi shuō, zhǐshì dì'èr tiān yòu mǎi le yī dài kāfēidòu, shàngmiàn yòng jìhaobǐ xiě le sì gè dà zì: 「Qǐng wù rēng diào」. Cóng nà yǐhòu, bīngxiāng shàng de zhǐtiáo yuè lái yuè shǎo le——bú shì wèntí jiějué le, ér shì dàjiā dōu xuéhuì le zài fàng dōngxi zhīqián xiān xiě shàng zìjǐ de míngzì hé rìqī. Yǒu shíhou, yī chǎng hùnluàn zuìzhōng néng cuīshēng chū zhìxù, zhǐshì guòchéng bú tài tǐmiàn.
text_en: The fridge in our office once sparked a 'war' that lasted three months. The cause was simple: someone ate someone else's yogurt. Xiao Wang, the victim, stuck a note on the fridge: 'Please don't touch my yogurt. Thank you.' The next day, another carton was missing. Xiao Wang posted another: 'My yogurt has my name on it. Please check before taking.' On the third day, someone replied beneath the note: 'Your yogurt is really delicious. Next time please buy two.' From then on, the fridge door became the entire office's message board. People complained about leftover food smelling too strong, demanded that stinky tofu be thrown out, and proposed everyone chip in for fruit. The best part was Sister Zhang from the admin department, who every Friday before leaving would clear out everything in the fridge older than three days. She called this 'Fridge Judgment Day.' Once she accidentally threw out the boss's coffee beans, and the whole office broke into a cold sweat on her behalf. But the boss said nothing—just bought another bag the next day with four large characters written in marker: 'DO NOT THROW AWAY.' After that, the notes on the fridge dwindled—not because the problem was solved, but because everyone learned to write their name and date before putting anything inside. Sometimes chaos eventually gives birth to order; it's just that the process isn't very dignified.

questions.q_zh: 「冰箱之战」的起因是什么？
questions.q_en: What started the 'fridge war'?

title: The Locksmith Who Knows Every Door
text_zh: 我们小区门口有个开锁的师傅，大家都叫他老马。他的摊子就是一辆改装过的电动三轮车，上面挂满了各种钥匙和锁具，看起来像一棵长满金属果实的树。老马干这行二十多年了，据说这附近五公里内的门锁，他闭着眼睛都能打开。有一次我锁在门外面了，打电话给老马，他问了我一句：「你住哪栋？」我说了门牌号，他想了两秒钟说：「你家是那种老式的弹子锁，对吧？」到了以后，不到三分钟就开了。我问他怎么记得这么清楚，他笑着说：「你在一个地方待久了，就会知道它的纹路。」后来聊天我才知道，老马不只是记锁。他知道哪栋楼的老人经常忘带钥匙，哪家的门锁该换了一直没换，哪些租户搬走了、新来的是什么样的人。他对这个社区的了解，细致到让我吃惊。有人说他是个没什么技术含量的手艺人，但我觉得不是。他做的事情其实和医生很像——都是通过长年累月的经验，读懂一个东西最隐秘的结构。只不过医生读的是人的身体，老马读的是门。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū ménkǒu yǒu gè kāisuǒ de shīfu, dàjiā dōu jiào tā lǎo Mǎ. Tā de tānzi jiùshì yī liàng gǎizhuāng guò de diàndòng sānlúnchē, shàngmiàn guà mǎn le gè zhǒng yàoshi hé suǒjù, kàn qǐlái xiàng yī kē zhǎng mǎn jīnshǔ guǒshí de shù. Lǎo Mǎ gàn zhè háng èrshí duō nián le, jùshuō zhè fùjìn wǔ gōnglǐ nèi de ménsuǒ, tā bì zhe yǎnjīng dōu néng dǎkāi. Yǒu yī cì wǒ suǒ zài mén wàimiàn le, dǎ diànhuà gěi lǎo Mǎ, tā wèn le wǒ yī jù: 「Nǐ zhù nǎ dòng?」Wǒ shuō le ménpáihào, tā xiǎng le liǎng miǎozhōng shuō: 「Nǐ jiā shì nà zhǒng lǎoshì de dànzǐ suǒ, duì ba?」Dào le yǐhòu, búdào sān fēnzhōng jiù kāi le. Wǒ wèn tā zěnme jì de zhème qīngchu, tā xiào zhe shuō: 「Nǐ zài yī gè dìfang dāi jiǔ le, jiù huì zhīdào tā de wénlù.」Hòulái liáotiān wǒ cái zhīdào, lǎo Mǎ bù zhǐ shì jì suǒ. Tā zhīdào nǎ dòng lóu de lǎorén jīngcháng wàng dài yàoshi, nǎ jiā de ménsuǒ gāi huàn le yīzhí méi huàn, nǎxiē zūhù bān zǒu le, xīn lái de shì shénme yàng de rén. Tā duì zhège shèqū de liǎojiě, xìzhì dào ràng wǒ chījīng. Yǒu rén shuō tā shì gè méi shénme jìshù hánliàng de shǒuyìrén, dàn wǒ juéde bú shì. Tā zuò de shìqíng qíshí hé yīshēng hěn xiàng——dōu shì tōngguò chángnián lěiyuè de jīngyàn, dú dǒng yī gè dōngxi zuì yǐnmì de jiégòu. Zhǐ búguò yīshēng dú de shì rén de shēntǐ, lǎo Mǎ dú de shì mén.
text_en: At the entrance to our housing compound there's a locksmith everyone calls Old Ma. His setup is a modified electric tricycle, hung all over with keys and lock parts—it looks like a tree bearing metal fruit. Old Ma has been in the trade for over twenty years. Rumor has it he could open any lock within five kilometers with his eyes closed. Once I got locked out, and when I called him, he asked just one question: 'Which building are you in?' I gave him the door number. He thought for two seconds and said: 'You've got one of those old pin tumbler locks, right?' After arriving, he had it open in under three minutes. I asked how he remembered so clearly. He smiled and said: 'When you've been in a place long enough, you learn its grain.' Chatting later, I discovered Old Ma doesn't just remember locks. He knows which building's elderly residents often forget their keys, whose door locks should have been replaced ages ago, which tenants have moved out, and what kind of people the new ones are. The detail of his understanding of this community startled me. Some people say he's a craftsman with no real technical sophistication, but I disagree. What he does is actually very similar to a doctor—both use accumulated experience over many years to read the most hidden structure of a thing. It's just that a doctor reads the human body, and Old Ma reads doors.
